metadata.title: Research Predictive vs Prognostic Biomarkers with Claude: Why KRAS G12C Is Not EGFR
metadata.color_semantics: teardown: INK #2A1A0E body text; CRIMSON #C8102E key finding/vulnerability; SLATE #545454 structure/neutral
metadata.note: CLI video REQUIRED spine: B00 intro · B01 PROBLEM · B02 ASK · B03 CODE · B04 OUTPUT(manim) · B05 CHANGE · B06 OUTPUT(slate) · B07 SUMMARY · B08 NEXT STEPS · B09 outro. Source: cancer-research/chapters/05-precision-oncology-matching-therapy-to-tumor.md
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Teardown register (voices/teardown/VOICE.md + CLAUDE-BRAND.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)

then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

beats.narration_text: Nik Bear Brown. Same mutation. Same drug. Two different failures.

Two patients with KRAS G12C get sotorasib. One responds for four months then progresses. The other never responds at all. Same biomarker, same drug, two different failures. Meanwhile EGFR exon-19 patients on osimertinib respond at over sixty percent and stay in response for years. What does the match actually guarantee — and why is KRAS G12C not EGFR?
beats.visual_intent: SLATE — problem card: KRAS G12C vs EGFR exon-19 response gap; same biomarker logic, different biology

beats.narration_text: In the terminal: ask Claude to compare KRAS G12C and EGFR exon-19 across response rate, durability, resistance mechanism, and oncogene addiction.

props.command:
claude "Compare KRAS G12C inhibition (sotorasib/adagrasib)
to EGFR exon-19 deletion inhibition (osimertinib)
across four dimensions: (1) response rate in the
indicated population, (2) typical durability of
response, (3) most common resistance mechanisms,
(4) what these differences reveal about relative
oncogene addiction. End with a one-sentence claim
about what distinguishes a good actionable alteration
from a merely present one. Cite published trial data."

beats.role_note: terminal (Onda) — Python script reading Claude response
beats.narration_text: Read the comparison. EGFR exon-19 with osimertinib: response rate over sixty percent, median PFS eighteen months, resistance via on-target C797S mutation. KRAS G12C with sotorasib: response rate thirty-seven percent, median PFS six months, resistance via multiple co-occurring pathways. The addiction difference is the explanation.

props.code:
# biomarker_compare.py -- Claude Code output
ALTERATIONS = [
  {"name": "EGFR exon-19 del",
   "drug": "osimertinib",
   "orr": ">60%",  "pfs": "18.9 mo (FLAURA2)",
   "resist": "C797S on-target; limited bypass",
   "addiction": "HIGH — tumor depends on EGFR signal",
   "verdict": "Strong actionable alteration"},
  {"name": "KRAS G12C",
   "drug": "sotorasib",
   "orr": "37%",   "pfs": "6.3 mo (CodeBreaK 100)",
   "resist": "KRAS amp, bypass via RTKs, MET, PI3K",
   "addiction": "PARTIAL — RAS signaling is not the only driver",
   "verdict": "Modest actionable alteration; context-dependent"},
]
# Key insight:
# Good alteration = tumor so dependent on that one signal
# that blocking it is like pulling the load-bearing column.
for a in ALTERATIONS:
    print(f"{a['name']}: ORR={a['orr']} PFS={a['pfs']}")
    print(f"  Addiction: {a['addiction']}")

beats.narration_text: The trajectories from the same starting tumor burden. EGFR osimertinib: a steep durable descent — tumor burden falls and stays down. KRAS G12C sotorasib: a V-shape — initial response then progression as bypass mechanisms activate. Non-responder: flat or rising throughout. Same axis, three different stories of oncogene dependence.
beats.visual_intent: Manim: B04_TumorTrajectories — three diverging tumor-burden lines from one starting point; labeled by alteration

beats.narration_text: Add a fourth trajectory: BRAF V600E across melanoma versus colorectal cancer — same mutation, radically different response rate.

props.command:
claude "Add the BRAF V600E story: same mutation in
melanoma (vemurafenib ORR ~50%) vs colorectal
cancer (vemurafenib monotherapy ORR <5%). Same
mutation, radically different response. What is
the biological explanation for the tissue-context
difference?"

beats.narration_text: BRAF V600E in melanoma: EGFR is not highly expressed, so BRAF inhibition hits the main driver. In colorectal cancer: EGFR is upregulated, provides a feedback bypass that reactivates MAPK signaling within hours of BRAF inhibition. The mutation is the same; the tissue context changes which bypass is available. Tissue context is a validity condition.
beats.visual_intent: SLATE — BRAF tissue-context contrast: melanoma vs CRC EGFR feedback bypass mechanism

beats.narration_text: The molecular match is a prediction. The response is the test. Matched and responded are different measurements separated by tumor evolution, co-occurring alterations, and the biology of oncogene dependence. A good actionable alteration is one where the tumor is so dependent on that one signal that blocking it is like pulling the load-bearing column.
beats.visual_intent: SLATE — summary card: match vs response; oncogene addiction as the governing variable

beats.narration_text: When evaluating a targeted therapy match, ask two questions beyond the alteration: how addicted is this tumor type to that specific signaling node, and what bypass mechanisms are available in this tissue context? The alteration identifies the target; those two questions predict whether hitting it will matter.
beats.visual_intent: SLATE — next steps: addiction question + bypass question; tissue context checklist

beats.narration_text: Nik Bear Brown. Build it with a CLI. Then take it apart. At Nik Bear Brown, nikbearbrown dot com.
